coredata.dc:title: A quantification method for Fe based particle contaminants in high purity materials for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ardia, Paola
Stallone, Salvatore
Cericola, Dario
coredata.dc:description: The technological growth, the increasing safety, and production standards cascade down to the quality of the raw materials. The development of analytical methods for high quality powder analysis needs to grow parallel to this trend. It is established that particle metal contaminants could trigger violent failures of lithium-ion batteries and compromise the battery performance, and the safety of the end user. Increased miniaturization requires exponential increases in quality of raw materials putting laboratories in a continuous process of development to achieve ever higher standards. Trace elements in a powder material can be present in different states, and their effect on the application can therefore be different according to the status. Trace elements can be present in the material as a dissolved impurity or as a particle contamination. Here we present an experimental method which is easy to implement and use, and is sensitive to contaminants (i.e. external particles that are not part of the material of interest) over total trace elements. Contaminants typically are a few orders of magnitude lower in concentration than total trace elements. The method focuses on the quantification of Fe, Ni and Cr in high purity carbon powders used in the lithium-ion battery industry. We present test sample size calculations prior to the method description to ensure high precision and assess possible bias. The method is based on the magnetic extraction of a large test sample, followed by acid digestion and quantification. The procedure and the method performance are described. The method ensures low levels of detection and quantification, high precision, and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe-contaminants
Quantification of trace contents
Li-ion battery
High quality powder

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Limit of detection and limit of quantitation
I. Magnet coating evaluation in aqua regia

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the method process.
Simulated position of the contaminant particles of different size in 1000g of powder material. The numbers of particles found in the first bin for the different test sample sizes are reported.

Box plots reporting C
s values (ppb) from the simulation as a function of m
s (g). C
s value was calculated 100 times for each m
s value. A) for powder material with C
t of 100 ppb, B) after the addition of 1 μg of cross contamination in m
s .
Boxplot of Fe, Cr and Ni of the ten tests for each powder and the blank (Table 2).

A) recovery fraction of two subsequent magnetic extractions on the same dispersion of Fe contaminants as a function of the amount of the contaminant Fe of the test sample (ppb). B) Boxplot of the recovery fraction of Fe in Fe-spiked powder matrixes as a function of the Fe particle size (μm).
Average sample blank values of ten independent measurements for Fe, Cr, Ni, and Sm and calculated standard deviations, which allows LOD and LOQ calculations.

row.entry: Average value of the sample blank

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
s0 : standard deviation,
s 0 ′
: standard error for population n.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of statistical analysis of the quantified powders and sample blanks allowing the boxplot representation in Fig. 4.
Average results for the three powders for the total Fe impurity contents (ppm), the quantified Fe contaminants (ppb) with the presented method and the ratio between the two quantifications (%).

head.ce:title: A quantification method for Fe based particle contaminants in high purity materials for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research & Development, Imerys Graphite & Carbon, 6804, Bironico, Switzerland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research & Development, Imerys Graphite and Carbon, 6804, Bironico, Switzerland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The technological growth, the increasing safety, and production standards cascade down to the quality of the raw materials. The development of analytical methods for high quality powder analysis needs to grow parallel to this trend. It is established that particle metal contaminants could trigger violent failures of lithium-ion batteries and compromise the battery performance, and the safety of the end user.

Increased miniaturization requires exponential increases in quality of raw materials putting laboratories in a continuous process of development to achieve ever higher standards. Trace elements in a powder material can be present in different states, and their effect on the application can therefore be different according to the status. Trace elements can be present in the material as a dissolved impurity or as a particle contamination.

Here we present an experimental method which is easy to implement and use, and is sensitive to contaminants (i.e. external particles that are not part of the material of interest) over total trace elements. Contaminants typically are a few orders of magnitude lower in concentration than total trace elements. The method focuses on the quantification of Fe, Ni and Cr in high purity carbon powders used in the lithium-ion battery industry. We present test sample size calculations prior to the method description to ensure high precision and assess possible bias. The method is based on the magnetic extraction of a large test sample, followed by acid digestion and quantification. The procedure and the method performance are described. The method ensures low levels of detection and quantification, high precision, and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: The technological growth of the manufacturing industry is constantly triggering the development of new materials, which in some properties are better performing than predecessors. The goal is to improve the performance of the final goods, reduce scrap, optimize production, and costs. The analytical methods to characterise those materials need to be developed alongside the material improvements in order to measure and control the required characteristics. The interest in recycled materials for higher sustainability of the battery industry is growing. Metals and other material parts from the lithium ion batteries could be recycled and reused to produce new batteries [1–3] increasing the sustainability and reducing the environmental impact.

The electronic and battery industries demand very high purity materials, with characterized trace elements contents as these might significantly affect production processes, product performance, and safety. For example, lithium-ion battery failures tend to be more severe than other rechargeable battery chemistries and affect reliability and safety. The high energy density can generate high temperatures and the flammable organic solvents can ignite and further increase heat [4]. Generally most failures followed by thermal runaway are results of cell internal faults. Internal faults may occur for various reasons such as dendrite growth, Li-plating, separator failure, mechanical damage, or operational abuse [4,5]. It is established that these failure mechanisms can be triggered by contaminant particles somehow introduced in the process stream of the lithium-ion cell [4 – 8].

Raw powder materials for lithium-ion battery production need to be carefully controlled for possible sources of metallic contaminants. Quantification of the metallic contaminants in powder has become increasingly important to prevent cell failure and, in some cases, even hazardous internal thermal runaways in batteries. Therefore manufacturing flaws and the quality of starting materials play a key role in the introduction of contaminants, which could lead to internal short circuits. The requirements in the battery-industry have consequently increased and the raw material suppliers need to develop higher purity products and introduce more rigorous controls [9].

Trace elements in a sample can be present in different states and their effect on the application can be different according to the status. A given element can be present in the powder material somehow dissolved or distributed homogeneously at the microscopic level, or it can be present as a foreign particle. We define the first case as impurity. The second case differs since the particle of the trace element is randomly distributed and ideally it can be identified and separated. This form is inhomogeneous [10] and we defined it as contaminant. The total concentration of a trace element, either as a particle contaminant or dissolved in the material, should be defined as the total impurity. Contaminants are then the particles randomly distributed in the powder, with a different composition than the material itself.

The demand of a growing industry for high quality materials indicates an indispensable need of a reliable method to quantify contaminants in powders. The problem of the speciation of micronic or sub-micronic particles in different matrices has been addressed by different authors [11–14]. The main concern relays in the high effort required to analyze enough material to be representative of the material of interest, resulting in difficult implementation. Many individual reports on aging of lithium-ion batteries from the various players are often poorly described and validated, which hinder a proper overview [15]. To the best of our knowledge there is not an analytical method generally accepted by the scientific and industrial community and little published literature to quantify the contaminants in powder at the typical concentration level present in high purity materials [10].

Poozhikunnath et al. [10] demonstrated in a study on carbon black that Fe-based magnetic particles are the majority of inhomogeneous contaminants. They specifically refer to large particles (10 μm), which could results in internal short circuits and catastrophic failure. Here we provide an easy to use method to quantify the Fe-based magnetic metallic contaminants in high purity carbon powders for the lithium-ion industry independently from their particle sizes. We developed a method which is easy to implement in industry and in a laboratory, and capable of providing representative information. The method we propose focuses on the quantification of Fe contaminants as well as Cr and Ni, which might be present as alloy elements together with Fe. Contaminants based on Fe are conductive and reactive particles that might have a severe impact on the lithium-ion cell, it is therefore important to have a reliable method for their quantification.

The analytical method is based on the extraction of the magnetic particles present in the test sample of powder material with a magnet. The particles are then dissolved through an acid digestion step. Finally, the acid solution is analysed by ICP-MS and the elements are quantified.

Fig. 1 shows the successive steps of the method to quantify the magnetic contaminants and provide validated results. All steps will be described. The possible sources of uncertainty of this method were investigated in this work. Random and systematic sampling error sources [16] are addressed through simulation, while random and systematic analytical errors are investigated within the validation of the method (see paragraph 3).

To reduce systematic analytical errors and to measure low ppb contents via ICP-MS of the diluted digested solutions it is imperative to proceed with maximum cleanliness during the entire analysis and using high purity materials and clean components. The large PE containers were repeatedly rinsed and washed with water, magnetic particles were removed, and used only for a specific powder. All the other containers, including PP containers and centrifuge tubes with PE-HD screw lids, were cleaned prior to use with HNO3 5% for at least 24h and properly stored in laminar flow cabinets (HEPA 14). Pipettes were rinsed twice with HNO3 5% before use [17]. Unless otherwise stated the water used was high purity water with resistivity >18.2 MΩ cm (Milli-Q ® - Merck) filtered at 0.22 μm. All the materials were handled with dedicated clean plastic (PP or PE) tools, and stored in dedicated clean plastic containers (PP, PE, or PTFE). All sensitive steps where materials are exposed to the environment are performed in a fume hood in a laboratory with F7 filtered air in order to protect the operator and minimize cross-contamination from the environment.

The size of the test sample plays a significant role in the precision and accuracy of the analytical results. Contaminants are particles randomly distributed in the powder material; therefore the test sample will contain a finite number of contaminant particles. The larger the test sample, the greater the number of contaminant particles included and thus analysed. Contaminant particles might have different sizes and the number might be different in function of the size. It is likely that large contaminant particles will be less common in the powder material than small contaminant particles. However, a large contaminant particle will have a bigger contribution to the total contaminant concentration than a small particle. It is therefore important that the test sample is large enough to collect a representative number of particles in relation to the desired level of precision. To identify a suitable test sample size we modelled the system with the objective to understand sampling precision and sampling bias as a function of the mass of the test sample.

An amount of powder material (of mass m t) is large enough that the distribution of contaminant particles is considered homogeneous. The number of contaminant particles in the powder material m t is
i
m t
. Test samples of mass m
s << m
t are generated out of this powder material. The number of particles in a given test sample (
i
m s
) might differ from another test sample of the same mass due to the nature of the random distribution of the particles, but always significantly smaller than in the powder material (
i
m s
≪
i
m i
).

The i
th contaminant particle is defined by its mass μ and size σ. The concentration C
t of contaminants in the powder material is therefore the sum of the masses μ
i,σ of all particles of the powder material (
i
m t
) of all sizes present in m
t divided by m
t
(1)
C t
=
∑
0 < i ≤
i
m z
, 0 < σ < ∞
μ
i , σ
m t
while the concentration C
s of contaminants in the test sample is
(2)
C s
=
∑
0 < i ≤
i
m s
, 0 < σ < ∞
μ
i , σ
m s

C
s and C
t are the true values of the concentration in the test sample and in the powder material, respectively.

The information of interest is C
t as the concentration of the powder material. The value of C
t cannot be determined experimentally as it would require the analysis of the entire m
t , therefore m
s has to be chosen in order that its C
s value is representative for C
t within the desired level of precision.

Calculations are performed assuming a given particle size distribution for the contaminants f(σ) defined between 10 and 200 μm. The distribution has a monotonous decay and was assumed by previous image analysis of the Fe-based magnetic contaminants. A random distribution within the powder material (i.e. a random mass value at which the particle is ideally encountered) is then assigned to each contaminant particle to model the powder material, sketched in Fig. 2 for a small portion of powder material. The value of C
t is set to 100 ppb of contaminant for the entire calculation.

The test sample is simulated by dividing the powder material in bins of size m
s . The number of bin replicas is set to 100 for every m
s value that is considered. The concentration C
s is calculated for each of the 100 bins of mass m
s . Fig. 3 A reports the value of C
s as a function of ms to assess the uncertainty associated with the test sample size.

The simulation results indicate that the scatter of C
s differ significantly as a function of m
s (Fig. 3 A). Small test samples provide a very large data scatter with a relative standard deviation (RDS) of 100% or more. In addition small test sample-sizes underestimate the median value of the distribution of the 100 bins. This is due to the likelihood of having larger particles in small sized test samples.

A sampling bias could be caused by a systematic error in the sampling procedure. As the handling of the test sample is similar regardless of its size, it is fair to assume that the same quantity of cross-contaminants could be introduced in the system while handling. We simulated therefore the effect of adding 1 μg of cross-contaminants in the powder sample m
s and repeating the previous calculations (this exercise could be extended to the whole measurement chain). The results are plotted in Fig. 3 B. The effect of the cross-contamination is more pronounced for small test sample size.

The calculations that are reported were conducted with the aim to define the size of the test sample. As previously mentioned, C
t and C
s are the true values, and the contaminant particle size distribution was assumed. These assumptions might have an important impact on the numerical results, however the overall message would not change. The calculations suggest that the test sample size should be as large as possible to increase precision and reduce sampling bias. As a best compromise between desired precision and manual handling of the test sample, we selected 500g as a test sample size and developed the analytical method accordingly. Based on the calculation, a 500g test sample should lead to RDS of 20%.

Three test carbon powders A, B, and C with particle sizes below 40 μm were investigated. The Fe content as total impurity was measured with spark discharge OES (DIN 51008 -1) and ranged between 7 and 62 ppm.

The Fe based magnetic particles are collected from the test sample through magnetic extraction. The test sample is first dispersed in water and a wetting agent, which deagglomerates all the particles and therefore frees the contaminant particles from the matrix. Acetone is an effective non-foaming wetting agent for carbon materials, and we used it in a ratio of 1–10 with water giving a solid content of 4.5 wt% (see details below). This value was empirically defined in order to have sufficiently low viscosity and better particle mobility. A magnetic extraction in wet phase is preferred over extraction from dry powder. Fine powders are often cohesive [18], reducing flowability and the chances for a magnetic particle to be collected by the magnet.

The extraction requires a permanent high strength magnet and a large external surface to ensure an effective collection of particles. To ensure durability of the magnetization to several heating cycles we selected Sm2Co17 28H magnets, with diameter of 20 mm, length of 50 mm with longitudinal magnetization and 1.6 10−5 (Vsm) (Arnold Magnetic Technologies GmbH, CH).

During the entire process the magnet is in contact with acetone or aqua regia (20 wt%). The magnet needs protection from corrosion and to avoid cross-contamination arising from dissolution of the magnetic material. The magnetic alloy used other than Sm and Co contains ca. 5 wt% Fe, which is one of the elements of interest, Zr, and Cu, which might be present in the magnetic materials. Rare earth elements used in the magnets are not found in the powder materials. Therefore, to validate the data results, possible cross-contamination needs to be monitored and tracked for each test solution. We choose to quantify Sm as analyte in the test solution as a control of the coating integrity as not present in the analysed powder carbon materials.

A suitable coating (Appendix I) with low porosity in E-CTFE (Halar, by HÜNI GmbH + Co. KG, D) of 1 mm allows acid and temperature protection of the fragile magnet. Each new magnet was first washed with water, then placed in a Teflon becher with cover and cleaned in aqua regia 20 wt% for 2 h at 70 °C. The magnet is then rinsed with water and placed in a clean PP vessel of 120 ml with PE-HD tight lid for later use. Used magnets after complete digestion may be reused and therefore rinsed with water and also placed in new clean vessels with a tight lid.

The test sample is placed in a clean 15 L drum (PE, wide neck) and then 10 L of water and 1 L of acetone (see Appendix II) are added gently to perfectly wet the powder to a dispersion. A clean magnet is added into the drum, then tightly closed and rotated long enough to assure that all magnetic contaminants stick to the magnet surface. Specifically, the drum is rotated on a jar roller for 4h at 25 rpm, as empirically identified. We assume that the gentle rotation allows the contaminant particle, when close to the magnet, to stay long enough in its proximity to be extracted. The sample blank tests proceeding were exactly as those with test powders, but without carbon powder (3.1 below). At the end, the drum is opened and the magnet is carefully recovered without interaction, by sliding a second magnet from the outside of the drum, rinsed with water to remove powder residues and placed in a clean PP-vessel awaiting the digestion step (see below).

The magnetic contaminants collected during the extraction and immobilized on the surface of the magnet are digested by placing the magnet in strong acids. The recovered magnets were placed in Teflon cylinders with a cover (internal diameter 30 mm, internal height. 100 mm, thickness 8 mm). Fresh aqua regia at 20 wt% was prepared using high purity acids (HCl 34–37% by NORMATOMⓇ for trace metal analysis and HNO3 67–69% by NORMATOMⓇ for metal trace analysis) and 25 mL were added into the cylinder to cover the coated magnet. Up to eight Teflon cylinders were placed on a heating plate with stainless-steel support and were heated with a bain-marie method to 70 °C. The duration of the digestion was defined empirically. The concentration of the analites did not change significantly after 30′ of digestion. The duration of the digestion was cautiously set to 60’. Afterward, the digested solution at 20 wt% was sampled and diluted to a 2 wt% solution with dedicated clean pipettes in a clean 50 ml PP-centrifuge tube and closed with a PE-HD screw lid for further analysis via ICP-MS. The magnet was rinsed with water and then stored in a new clean 120 ml vessel for further tests.

ICP-MS analysis allows the quantification of low amounts of trace to ultratrace elements in solutions with different matrices [19]. The ICP-MS system was an Agilent 7800 quadropole, equipped with MassHunter workstation software (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA). The instrument was tuned daily using auto-tuning; the tuning solution consisted of 1 ppb of each Li, Y, Ce, and Tl in 2%HNO3. Relative standard deviations lower than 2.5%, were deemed satisfactory. The ratio of doubly charged to singly charged ions and the oxide ratio in He-mode were maintained below 1%. Elements included in the analysis were; in no gas: Li, B, Al; Sc; in He mode: Al, Sc, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Sr, Ag, Cd, In, Ba, Sm, Tb, Lu,Tl, [Pb]206, [Pb]207,Pb208, Bi. One point peak with three replica measurements were taken for each element, with Sc, Nb and Lu as the instrument internal standards (ISTD). Integration time on “no gas” mode was 0.f 0.1 s, on “He-mode” was of 0.3 s and 0.5 for Fe and 1.0 s for As and Cd. The expected concentration of analyte in the solutions is 0–1200 ppb. To decrease the limits of analyte detection the physical interface should be reduced. We used a common matrix for all the solutions of aqua regia at 2 wt% and all the standards were fresh for each session, which allowed us to increase the ISTD recovery. The spectral interference was checked at the beginning of the study for the analyte and monitored during data evaluation.

Each analytical session began with a series of calibration solutions (diluted from standards manufactured by VWR, see Appendix II) based on aqua regia 2 wt%. The validity of the standards calibration is checked at the beginning of each ICP-MS session, and improved when possible with particular focus on the interesting elements as Fe, Ni, Cr and Sm quantified in He-mode. The calibration solutions used allowed the quantification of 21 elements based with a blank and seven solutions ranging from 1 to 1′000 ppb. The calibration of the elements of particular interest (Fe, Ni, Cr and Sm) were closely monitored with R between 1 and 0.9998 and regularly measured. The ISTD recovery was always measured and results were within ±15%. Analysis variations of the ISTD recovery were punctually checked and in case reanalysed or removed.

As previously mentioned, one of the most likely sources of cross-contamination is the magnet itself, as it contains ca. 5 wt% of Fe. To validate data results, possible cross-contamination is monitored and tracked for each test solution. Sm content in the test solution monitors the coating integrity. As measured Sm values over 0.5 ppb were detected in a diluted test solution (ca. 0.22 ppb of the test sample), then the entire test sample, was rejected and the magnet used disposed of. Sm values just above the LOQ value of 0.07 ppb (Table 1 ) and 0.22 ppb in the test sample were double checked (i.e Sm in the diluted solution between 0.15 and 0.5 ppb).

Once the digested solution data obtained (
C
I C P − M S
in ppb) were validated, the magnetic metallic contaminants in the three test-powders and in the blanks were calculated (C
sm in ppb) following equation (3). The sample blanks were calculated assuming an initial powder of 500 g (M
sm ).

C

]

M
s m
with: C
ms the calculated concentration of contaminants in the sampled powder (ppb); C
ICP-MS the concentration for the element measured via ICP-MS in (ppb); V
dig. the volume (ml) of aqua regia for digestion, Acidity
dig the acidity of the digestion solution (%, i.e. 20%), Acidity
ICP-MS the acidity (%, i.e. 2%) of the aqua regia for the ICP-MS analysis (dilution factor of 10:1), and M
sm the initial powder weight (g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection and limit of quantitation
ce:section.ce:para:
Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were calculated through the analysis of n repetitions of sample blanks. Ten independent sample blanks were analysed. The sample blanks are providing the total “blank” resulting from the reagent blank (see specifics in appendix II), cleaned containers and environmental background. Table 1 reports the standard deviations for Fe, Cr, Ni, and Sm measured on the blank samples, as well as LOD calculated as
s 0 ′
* 3
, and LOQ calculated as
s 0 ′
* 10
according to Ramsey et al. [16].

The assessment of the experimental precision of the method was done by measuring several replicated measurements on the same materials. Test samples were produced from 3 different carbon materials. All the test samples were then analysed in conditions of intermediate reproducibility, which in this specific case means that all the measurements were done in the same laboratory, with the same equipment, by different operators over a period of time of 6 months.

Table 2 reports the details of the number of readings, concentration values and RDS for three materials of significantly different contamination levels. Fe is the most relevant element quantified. Cr, and Ni likely come together as elements alloyed with Fe. Precision is ca. 30% for Fe over the measurement range.

The calculation reported in chapter 2.1 gives a RDS value for the test sample of 500g of ca. 20%. Considering the assumptions taken for the calculations (i.e. this RDS should be intended as sampling precision only) as well as the fact that the experimental RDS value is a combination of sampling as well as analytical uncertainties we find a good alignment between the RDS values. The calculations reported are a good tool to estimate the performance of the analytical method as a function of the test sample size and is confirmed by the experimental results.

Accuracy is typically quantified via the analysis of certified reference material (CRM). In accordance to the method proposed and to the best of our knowledge a suitable CRM for the proposed method does not exist. Such reference material should have a similar matrix and a certified concentration of Fe contaminants intended as Fe particles and not as Fe trace elements allowing the validation of the entire method proposed. It is therefore not possible to assess the accuracy with a traditional approach. We choose to investigate accuracy of the method by measuring the recovery on test samples of real-powders samples, and on spiked samples. We didn't consider spiking digested solutions [20] as the results would not investigate the extraction nor the digestion.

Recovery on test sample measurements was first calculated by the fraction of contaminant (Fe) collected on a first extraction divided by the sum of the contaminant (Fe) collected on two subsequent extractions with new magnets on the same carbon dispersion. The recovery data for Fe are plotted against test sample Fe concentrations in Fig. 5 A. Recovery was investigated in the range LOQ to ca. 600 ppb. The recovery clearly increases from ca. 0.5 at the LOQ to 0.9 at 600 ppb.

The recovery was also studied on powder samples spiked with pure Fe powders (1–6 μm and 98% Fe, <44 μm and 97% Fe, and <60 μm and 99% Fe, Sigma Aldrich now Merck group, St. Louis, Missouri, United States). As the contaminant was introduced externally, the effect of particle size of the contaminant on the recovery of the entire measurement method was also studied.

The amount of Fe introduced in the samples was between 100 and 600 ppb. The carbon used as a matrix was selected with a low contaminant concentration close to the detection limit (<15 ppb). In this case we did not observe a significant dependency of the recovery as a function of the contaminants concentration level in contrast to the test samples. Likely, the contaminant is bound differently to the matrix, and it could be speculated that in spiked samples the introduced contaminant is less bound to the matrix than in real samples. Fig. 5 B reports the experimental recovery of Fe, calculated as contaminant measured divided by contaminant introduced by spiking, as a function of the spiking Fe particle size. Results demonstrate that the larger the contaminant particle, the better the recovery.
Based on the measurement performed we did not observe anomalies in the analytical performance of the method in the range between LOQ and 7′000 ppb of Fe as contaminant (Table 1).

The method was intended to be selective for contaminants over total impurity. A strict assessment of selectivity on real test samples is difficult due to the nature of the matrix. It is not possible to vary the amount of possible interfering elements by spiking. An indication of the selectivity can be done by comparing the value obtained for the contaminant with the values of total impurities, which are summarized in Table 3 . Total impurity concentrations were determined by spark-discharge optical emission spectroscopy (DIN 51008 -1).

The ratio between contaminant concentration and total impurity concentration varies significantly between 0.03% and 11% among the materials that were analyzed. This suggests that contaminants and impurities are independent quantities. Taken that accuracy and precision are similar in the measurement range, the significant variation in the ratio suggests that the method is capable of quantifying contaminants regardless of the level of total impurity, and thus it is selective for contaminants over total impurities.

The analytical method presented in this work can be applied for the evaluation of Fe based magnetic contaminants present in high purity powder materials typically used in the electronic and battery industry. The rigorous characterization that was performed demonstrates the performance of the method and its applicability in this context.

The numerical simulation is an important tool to define test sample size. Test sample size has a critical impact on the analytical performance of the method. In this specific measurement chain, sampling precision has a larger contribution. As a general consideration the simulation results are not bound to a specific analytical method. It is necessary to use a large test sample size due to the intrinsically random distribution of contaminants in a powder sample and therefore to minimize to an acceptable magnitude the contribution of the sampling precision to the overall method precision. In our case handling of the sample and desired analytical quality led to the decision to use 500g as a test sample size.

The validity of the method is demonstrated for the defined 500 g sample size. The method was not validated with reference materials as a valid candidate is missing: The intermediate experimental precision is 30% RSD for Fe, which is consistent with the simulation precision of 20%. The accuracy estimated from the recovery ratio, varies between 50 and 90% and is dependent on the Fe contaminant content. Spiking recovery was independent of the contaminant content indicating a different behaviour as a function of the matrix.

The presented method is easy to implement and use for the various players in the lithium ion battery industry as well as high quality analytical facilities. It is proved reliable for quantifying Fe based magnetic contaminants over total iron trace element content in high purity carbon powders on a wide working range.

Paola Ardia: Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation and Review & Editing. Salvatore Stallone: Investigation. Dario Cericola: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The fruitful discussions with Michael Spahr, Jeremy Hooper, Luca Ostinelli and Enrico Ortelli are gratefully acknowledged. Sara Eyvindson is gratefully thanked for proofing the text. This study did not receive any specific funding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: I. Magnet coating evaluation in aqua regia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Magnet coating specifics and test findings.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Supplier: 1: Stanford Magnetics (USA); 2: HÜNI GmbH + Co. KG (D); 3: LAFONTE.EU S.R.L (I).

ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of the coating (App. table 1 type for the Sm2Co17 magnet is crucial to the contaminant extraction and digestion as well as the longevity of the magnets for further analyses. The coatings were first empirically evaluated as a function of their aqua regia resistance. Then an evaluation of the costs for the first coating and the thickness, which decreases the magnetic strength, was conducted.

The E-CTFE coating was finally chosen. On average, a magnet resisted more than 7 magnetic extraction cycles (equivalent to 28 h in diluted acetone) and more than 20 h in strong acid, aqua regia 20 wt%.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Details of the solution employed for the method validation, from extraction to the ICP-MS analysis (App. table 2).))

row.entry: Multi-element quality control standard 1.000 mg/l in nitric acid
Samarium Standard 1000 ppm in nitric acid

Lutetium ICP standard
1000 mg/l Lu in nitric acid

Terbium ICP standard
1000 mg/l Tb in nitric acid

Scandium ICP Standard
1000 mg/l Tb in nitric acid

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Internal analysis of Milli-Q ®. Resistivity >18.2 MΩ cm filtered at 0.22 μm.
cCarlo Erba Reagents S.A.S. F27106 Val de Reuil Cedex, France.
vVWR International GmbH, Lerzenstrasse 16/18; 8953 Dietikon, Switzerland.
